summarization.a: Ball-and-stick model of MeCpPtMe₃ chemisorbed on pristine graphene, showing adsorption geometry and minimal basal-plane distortion. Relative energy with respect to separated species is indicated.
summarization.b: Ball-and-stick model of MeCpPtMe₃ chemisorbed on graphene with a 585 divacancy defect, showing local structural distortion and altered adsorption geometry.
summarization.c: Ball-and-stick model of MeCpPtMe₃ chemisorbed on graphene with a bare grain boundary, illustrating adsorption-induced buckling along the boundary region.
summarization.d: Ball-and-stick model of MeCpPtMe₃ chemisorbed on epoxyated graphene, highlighting adsorption at oxygen-functionalized sites and local basal-plane distortion.
summarization.e: Ball-and-stick model of MeCpPtMe₃ chemisorbed on hydroxylated graphene, showing adsorption at hydroxyl-functionalized sites with induced structural buckling.
summarization.f: Ball-and-stick model of MeCpPtMe₃ chemisorbed on hydrogenated graphene grain boundaries, illustrating adsorption geometry and local surface distortion, with relative energies indicated.

a.question: Does functionalization of graphene grain boundaries influence the adsorption geometry of MeCpPtMe₃?
a.answer: Yes. Oxygenation or hydrogenation of grain boundaries causes buckling in the graphene basal plane, altering the chemisorption geometry of MeCpPtMe₃.

b.question: Which graphene surface type shows the most pronounced structural deformation upon MeCpPtMe₃ chemisorption?
b.answer: Functionalized grain boundaries (oxygenated or hydrogenated GBs) show the most pronounced buckling due to sp³ hybridization of carbon atoms.

d.question: How does the diversity of chemisorbed MeCpPtMe₃ species on different graphene surfaces impact potential ALD applications?
d.answer: The variation in adsorption geometries and the structural distortions caused by defects or functionalization indicate that Pt ALD will be highly surface-sensitive. Pristine graphene allows limited binding, while defective or functionalized surfaces promote stronger chemisorption. This suggests that controlled defect engineering or surface functionalization could be used to optimize Pt nucleation and growth in ALD processes, enhancing coverage and uniformity on graphene-based substrates.

c.question: What are the observed structural effects of chemisorbing MeCpPtMe₃ on different graphene surfaces?
c.answer: Buckling of the basal plane, Formation of folds or distortions at line defects, Local rehybridization of carbon bonds to sp³ at functionalized sites, Variation in adsorption geometry depending on surface composition
